twitter_thread: 70% of long-term stock portfolios are built on just 5 stocks, like Apple, Microsoft, and Johnson & Johnson. Is that enough?
Use the 60/30/10 rule: 60% in S&P 500 index funds, 30% in mid caps like Visa, and 10% in small caps like Shopify
Dollar-cost averaging can cut timing risk by 15%, but monthly investments can water down returns by 5% annually
The S&P 500 beat 82% of actively managed funds over 10 years, with an average annual return of 10.5%
Holding tax-inefficient assets like REITs in tax-deferred accounts can save 12% in taxes
Rebalancing quarterly can lead to 8% higher trading costs, consider semi-annual rebalancing instead
What's your top priority in your long-term stock portfolio: 8% annual growth, 4% dividend income, or 10% risk reduction? #stockmarket #investing
pack.linkedin: Most long-term investors rely on a handful of stocks like Apple, Microsoft, and Johnson & Johnson. But is that enough diversification? The 60/30/10 rule can provide a more balanced approach, allocating 60% to S&P 500 index funds, 30% to mid caps like Visa, and 10% to small caps like Shopify. Dollar-cost averaging can reduce timing risk, but implementing it monthly can decrease returns by 5% annually. Historically, the S&P 500 has outperformed 82% of actively managed funds over 10-year periods, with an average annual return of 10.5%. Tax efficiency is also crucial, with tax-deferred accounts saving 12% in taxes on tax-inefficient assets like REITs. What do you prioritize in your long-term stock portfolio: growth, income, or risk management?
tiktok_hooks: Just found out 70% of investors are putting all their eggs in just 5 baskets
Want to make your portfolio last? It's not about picking winners, it's about numbers

pack.reddit_headline: 5 stocks enough for long-term investing: yes or no?
